smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells a $9,750 mechanical spaceflight watch called Monolith with no reviews and no ads currently visible, so this call is about starting a considered-purchase funnel from zero, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watchmaker based in San Francisco. They frame themselves as a platform for space exploration tools, not a heritage luxury brand. Very early stage and mission-driven.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: One flagship product we can confirm: the Monolith, an ultra-premium mechanical toolwatch built for spaceflight, priced at $9,750 in three strap colorways (Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange). Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026 and orders are fully refundable before shipment. They also have a Memory Disc initiative and a Project One, plus downloadable non-commercial source files. Do not describe the Disc or Project One in detail, we do not have the facts.
deep_dive.positioning: Top of the market. Technical, mission-driven, frontier-minded. Not selling status, selling engineering and being early to a story about life beyond Earth.
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space and tech enthusiasts with high disposable income. People who value provable engineering, being early, and the frontier narrative. Small, specific, high-value buyers.
strengths: Strong, distinctive brand voice and story. A $9,750 watch about space is inherently scroll-stopping, which is rare and useful for cold ads.
High price point means one sale is worth a lot. You do not need volume to make paid ads pay back, you need a handful of the right buyers.
gaps: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. For a five-figure purchase from an unknown brand, that is a serious trust hole.
No Meta ads found running, so there is no existing winner to scale. We are building the funnel from scratch, and delivery does not start until Q4 2026, which lengthens the buying decision.

game_plan.headline: Build a warm audience around the Monolith story first, then convert the small pool of high-intent collectors into refundable pre-orders.

phases.goal: Prove that cold people will stop, watch, and click for this brand at all. Find one ad angle that earns cheap attention.
campaigns: One prospecting campaign to cold audiences testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against each other
A view and engagement capture so we can retarget everyone who watched or clicked next month
lead_products: The Monolith. It is the only product we can confirm and price, and it is the flagship the whole brand is built around. We lead with it because it is the story, not because we know it outsells anything else.
phases.creative: Run all 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Lead with the ad that carries the strongest story hook (the 'spaceship for your wrist' / window to the future angle), because a $9,750 watch is bought on story and engineering belief, not on discount. The other two test the engineering-proof angle and the frontier-early angle. Stay inside the brand rules: no NASA claims, no 'flown in space', no 'best in the world', no fake urgency.
phases.budget: Start small and steady. See estimate below.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad clearly beating the others on cheap clicks and video views, plus a growing pool of people we can retarget. We are NOT expecting sales yet at this price point.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning angle and start turning warm attention into leads and refundable pre-orders.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold ad and build 2 new versions off the same angle
A retargeting campaign to everyone who watched or clicked in month one, pushing newsletter signup and the fully refundable pre-order
lead_products: Monolith again, now split-tested by strap colorway to see which the market pulls toward (Barrelhand Orange vs Black vs Black & Orange).
phases.creative: Widen the cold audience around the winning angle. On retargeting, lean on the refundable pre-order to lower the risk of a five-figure commitment, and use the newsletter as a softer step for people not ready to buy.
phases.budget: Raise budget only on what won in month one.
phases.what_success_looks_like: First refundable pre-orders or a steadily growing qualified email list, and a clear read on which colorway and message people respond to.

phases.goal: Scale what is working and add a second offer to widen the funnel.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold and retargeting setup
Introduce a second product or offer to bring in people not ready for a $9,750 purchase (the Memory Disc, or a lower-commitment entry point) - pending what Karel tells us it actually is and its price
lead_products: Monolith as the hero, plus a second offer to be confirmed on the call. We cannot name the second product's price or details from public data, so this phase depends on discovery.
phases.creative: Best-performing Monolith ads at higher spend, plus fresh creative for the second offer once we know what it is.
phases.budget: Scale in line with what the earlier phases return.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A repeatable path from cold ad to pre-order that we can keep feeding, plus a second entry point bringing in cheaper leads we warm up over time.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get a few hundred clicks and thousands of video views a week, which is the floor for learning which of the 3 ads earns attention before we spend real money

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot honestly put a number on this before the call
estimates.basis: at $9,750 an order, one or two sales swings the whole result, and with no past ad data and delivery not starting until Q4 2026 we would be guessing. We need to know their past conversion history and margin first.

assumptions: The Monolith at $9,750 is the main thing they want to sell right now.
They can accept refundable pre-orders ahead of Q4 2026 delivery and are comfortable advertising a product that ships later.
There is real buyer intent for a five-figure independent space watch, which the long buying cycle and lack of reviews may slow down.
Karel told us they are already running Meta ads, but we found none live. The account status is unknown until he confirms it.

questions.question: You mentioned you're already running Meta ads. Can you walk me through what's live right now and what you're spending?
questions.listening_for: We could not find any live ads. Listen for whether they are actually running, paused, or were never really live. This tells you if you are fixing or starting from zero.

questions.question: Who's handling the ads today, you or an agency or someone in-house?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is an incumbent to replace, or nobody, or Karel doing it himself. Shapes how you position taking over.

questions.question: On a $9,750 watch, what does your margin look like after everything? Roughly what can you afford to pay to get one sale?
questions.listening_for: A real number for what a customer is worth. At this price there is likely plenty of room, but you need it to set the budget honestly.

questions.question: How many Monoliths have you sold so far, and how did those buyers find you?
questions.listening_for: Whether they have any sales at all yet, and whether sales came from ads, word of mouth, or press. This is the single most important fact you are missing.

questions.question: When someone does buy, how long does it usually take from first hearing about you to placing the order?
questions.listening_for: Long buying cycles are normal at five figures. If it is weeks or months, the plan leans harder on retargeting and email, not quick cold sales.

questions.question: There are no reviews or buyer stories on the site. Do you have any customers, press, or testimonials we could point to?
questions.listening_for: Any proof at all. If they have owners or press coverage, we can use it. If truly nothing, the ads have to carry all the trust and that changes the creative.

questions.question: When people land on the site but don't buy, what do you think stops them? Price, the Q4 2026 wait, or just not knowing you?
questions.listening_for: Their own read on the drop-off. Points you at whether the problem is trust, the delivery wait, or awareness.

questions.question: Are you set up to take fully refundable pre-orders now, and are you comfortable running ads for something that ships in Q4 2026?
questions.listening_for: Confirms the offer we can actually advertise. If pre-orders are off the table, the whole plan changes.

questions.question: When people search for you by name, or search things like independent space watch or spaceflight toolwatch, are you showing up?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is existing search demand for the category. Collectors do research five-figure watches heavily, so branded and category search is a genuine opening for Google and SEO, unlike a category nobody looks for.

questions.question: Do you get much press or forum chatter, and does that traffic ever turn into sales?
questions.listening_for: Watch collectors live in forums and read reviews. If there is demand there, SEO and content are worth raising honestly.

objections.objection: We're already running ads, why do I need you?
objections.answer: That's actually why I built you the mockups first. When I looked, I couldn't find any of your ads live, so I'd want to understand what's running before I say anything. If they are running and working, great, I'll tell you that. If they've stalled, starting clean with a tested angle is faster than patching.

objections.objection: A $9,750 watch is not an impulse buy, ads won't sell it.
objections.answer: Agreed, and that's exactly why month one isn't about sales. At this price nobody clicks buy from one ad. We use ads to find the people who lean in, then warm them up with retargeting and your newsletter, and lean on the fully refundable pre-order to lower the risk of committing before it ships.

objections.objection: We have no reviews, so how would ads even work?
objections.answer: That's the first thing I flagged too. It's your biggest gap right now, not just for ads but for the whole site. Early on the ads carry the engineering story and the frontier angle instead of star ratings, and one of the fastest wins is getting your first buyers and press quotes onto the site so we can point to them.

objections.objection: Deliveries don't start until Q4 2026, isn't it too early to spend on ads?
objections.answer: It's actually a good reason to start now. A five-figure watch takes months for someone to decide on. Building the warm audience and the pre-order list ahead of delivery means you're not starting cold when stock lands. The refundable pre-order lets people commit with zero risk in the meantime.

red_flags.flag: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Asking a stranger to spend $9,750 on an unknown brand with zero visible proof is a hard sell. It weakens ad performance and needs fixing alongside the ads.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads found live, despite Karel saying they run them.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is no existing winner to scale and no past data. Do not walk in assuming an account to optimize. Confirm what is actually running before promising anything.

red_flags.flag: Delivery does not start until Q4 2026.
red_flags.why_it_matters: You are selling something that ships later, which lengthens the decision and can spook buyers. The plan must rely on the refundable pre-order and a warm-up period, not fast sales.

red_flags.flag: Very high price and a tiny, specialist audience.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Results will be lumpy. One sale is huge, but a slow month is not proof the ads failed. Set that expectation early so nobody panics in week two.

other_channel_openings: Google search is a real opening. Serious collectors research five-figure watches heavily, and people who hear the brand will search it by name. Confirm on the call whether they show up for their own name and for category terms like independent space watch.

SEO and content have honest potential because the watch world runs on forums, reviews, and deep-dive articles. Worth raising only if Karel confirms there is search and forum interest, not as a forced add-on.
